caption.p: Illustration of focal traits and linked deleterious selection. (A) We study models in which phenotypic variation for a focal trait is caused by a large number of loci that are linked to loci which have deleterious effects via other fitness components. We are primarily interested in models where the focal trait is a disease evolving under the liability threshold model (B) or a quantitative trait under stabilizing selection (C).

Genetic diversity reduction for single-site additive directional selection model under background selection. (A) The predicted change in the contribution to heterozygosity conditional on not being removed via linkage to a BGS site (i.e. the ratio h(γB)h(γ) from equation (5a)). (B) The selective fixation rate, ρ(γ)-1, with and without BGS, over the range [−50,50] in the main plot and [−3,3] in the inset. (C) The solid black line shows the diversity reduction, Bγ, predicted by Equation (5), while the horizontal red dashed line shows the diversity reduction expected under neutrality, B. In simulations we used the chromosome structure shown on the top left (gray bars represent regions with BGS sites), varied the scaled selection coefficients of the focal sites and measured the average genetic diversity reduction over many replicates. In the insets (i)- (iv), the upper panels show the site frequency spectrum, scaled relative to its neutral expectation, for γ=-10, −1.5, 1.5, 25. The color indicates whether background selection is (blue) or is not (orange) operating. The solid line represents the predicted deleterious SFS with effective population size N; while the dashed line represents the predicted deleterious SFS with effective population size NB. The left violet dashed vertical line plots 12Nσ where σ is the average genome-wide standard deviation of the background fitness from simulations. The right purple dashed vertical line plots 12NBsbgs. Lower panels plot the density of alleles at each frequency under background selection compared to no background selection.

The impact of background selection in the liability threshold model. (A) The genetic variance reduction due to background selection for the exponential fitness function (black) and the threshold model (purple) in simulations. The schematic at top right illustrates the chromosome structure of these simulations, with trait loci (red bars) interspersed among BGS sites (grey regions) (B) The impact of background selection on disease prevalence in the single-effect liability threshold model, when pT≫12L. (B; top inset box) Increasing the strength of BGS shifts the mean toward the threshold and decreases the variance (B; main figure) The disease prevalence increases with stronger background selection. The theoretical prediction of Equation (25) is plotted in a black dashed line. Red dots correspond to averages over individual simulation runs, while the solid line connect means for each value of B. (C) In contrast, when the threshold is close to zero in the single-effect-size model (i.e. pT~12L), the liability distribution is not affected by background selection, as shown in the box. Consequently, the disease prevalence is not affected by increasing the background selection intensity. In (D), we simulated two-effect traits under the liability threshold model, with one of the effect sizes in the small-effect regime (as=1) and the other effect size in the large-effect regime. We varied the effect sizes of the large-effect alleles. (i) – (iii) illustrates how the risk effect size for the large-effect-size allele changes with background selection depending on its effect size. The region in red represents the proportion of diseased individuals no matter whether the individuals carry the large effect allele; orange represents the proportion of individuals that will develop the disease if they carry the large effect allele but will be healthy otherwise (risk effect size); and blue represents the proportion of healthy individuals no matter whether they carry the large effect allele. (i) The effect size is relatively small and the risk effect size can be linearly approximated with the darker orange region. (ii) The linearity does not hold and the risk effect size increase is larger than 1B. (iii) When the effect size has a similar scale as the phenotypic variation, the change in liability distribution has a negligible impact on risk effect size. (D) While the small-effect-size alleles (green) have a constant reduction in variance (approximately at a ratio of B), the reduction of variance varies for the large-effect-size allele (brown). With an increased effect size, the ratio drops below B-value and then increases to one. The theoretical predictions (Supplementary section B) of variance reduction for the small-effect and large-effect alleles are plotted in green and red dashed lines.

Stabilizing Selection. In (A), we simulated traits with a single constant effect size under stabilizing selection with chromosome structure shown on the top right. Across different settings, we varied effect sizes and measured the reduction in genetic variance due to background selection (y-axis). The x-axis represents the measured average selection coefficient at each causal locus. The method used for these estimations is detailed in Methods. The red dashed line is the expected genetic diversity reduction of a neutral allele at B=0.83. The blue solid line is the theoretical prediction of the genetic reduction of a single selected focal allele with a BGS intensity at B=0.83. The prediction aligns well with the single-locus simulation results, shown in the insect. To demonstrate that the size of increase in heterozygosity due to BGS depends on the magnitude of the BGS effect, we also plot the predicted reduction of genetic diversity with different BGS intensities in grey lines.

In (B) we plot the “relative” site frequency spectrum of the focal selected site with different selection coefficients (2Nt=1.5 on the left panel and 2Nt=5 on the right panel) observed in single-locus simulations. The y-axis is the number of sites at each frequency relative to the neutral expectation with θ=4Nμ. The color represents the scenario whether there is background selection or not. The solid line represents the predicted deleterious SFS with effective population size N; while the dashed line represents the predicted deleterious SFS with effective population size NB. The insets show the relative density of alleles at each frequency bin.

boxed-text.p: Physical linkage of genetic variants to nearby deleterious mutations shapes their evolution. The effect of this linkage is similar to a reduction in population size, because chromosomes carrying deleterious alleles are quickly removed. This leads to reduced genetic variation for neutral variants, and for variants under weak directional selection. Here, we analyze the impact of linkage in models of selection on complex, polygenic traits. We find that when selection acts on traits through a threshold, linkage reduces genetic variation for much more strongly selected sites than existing theory predicts. In contrast, if selection stabilizes trait values toward an optimum, then linkage may actually increase genetic variation. Understanding the fully effect of linkage therefore requires understand how selection acts on traits.
